attributes.stem:
A seven-year-old girl presents to the General Practice clinic with her mother who reports finding small insects in her daughter's hair while brushing it this morning. The girl has complained of an itchy scalp for the past three days. She is otherwise well. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. She attends the local primary school where several classmates have recently been treated for head lice. Her mother expresses reluctance to use medications for treatment.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Examination of her scalp is shown (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a classic case of head lice infestation (pediculosis capitis), characterized by the presence of live lice and nits (eggs) attached to hair shafts. Head lice are common in school-aged children and spread through direct head-to-head contact.

Wet combing with conditioner is considered an effective first-line treatment for head lice. This mechanical method involves applying conditioner to wet hair and systematically combing with a fine-toothed lice comb to remove both live lice and eggs. Research has shown that wet combing is as effective as chemical treatments when performed correctly. The procedure should be performed by first combing out tangles with a wide-toothed comb, then applying conditioner to dry hair, ensuring the roots are covered. A fine-toothed lice comb is placed flat against the scalp and drawn through each section of hair from roots to ends. The comb should be checked after each stroke and any lice removed. Each section should be combed at least five times. This process must be repeated every second day for 10 days (ie 5 treatments) to remove newly hatched lice. This method has the advantages of being non-toxic, inexpensive, and not contributing to insecticide resistance.

Permethrin 1% lotion is a common chemical treatment for head lice. While effective, it is not superior to wet combing when the latter is performed correctly and consistently. Chemical treatments also carry risks of skin irritation and allergic reactions. Given the mother's expressed reluctance to use medications, this option would not align with the family's preferences.

Head shaving is an extreme and unnecessary approach to head lice management. While it would effectively remove the lice and nits, it is psychologically traumatic for children and can lead to social stigmatisation and emotional distress. There are many effective, less drastic treatments available that should be tried first.

Oral ivermectin is not approved as a first-line treatment for head lice in most countries. It would only be considered in severe, treatment-resistant cases, particularly in individuals who cannot tolerate topical treatments. It is not appropriate for routine use in children with uncomplicated head lice infestations. This medication option would also conflict with the mother's expressed reluctance to use medications.

Tea tree oil shampoo has limited evidence supporting its effectiveness against head lice. While some studies suggest it may have some pediculicidal properties, it is not recommended as a primary treatment due to inconsistent formulations and insufficient clinical evidence. Additionally, tea tree oil can cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals.

In addition to treating the affected child, it's important to check all household members and close contacts for infestation, as only about 50% of people with head lice experience itching. Any family members with head lice should be treated simultaneously to break the infestation cycle. Environmental measures such as washing bedding and clothing used in the previous 48 hours in hot water may be helpful, though lice cannot survive for long periods away from the human host.
learningPoints: Head lice infestation affects people of all ages and socioeconomic groups, with only 50% of infested individuals experiencing itching.
Wet combing is as effective as chemical treatments like permethrin for treating head lice.
All close contacts of a person with head lice should be examined, regardless of symptoms.
references: RACGP Handbook of Non-Drug Interventions: Wet combing: head lice. First published: October 2013, Last updated: February 2021. Accessed at: https://www.racgp.org.au/clinical-resources/clinical-guidelines/handi/a-z/w/wet-combing-head-lice
Handbook Of Non Drug Intervention (HANDI) Project Team. Wet combing for the eradication of head lice. Aust Fam Physician. 2013 Mar;42(3):129-30. PMID: 23529522.
Frydenberg A, Starr M. Head lice. Aust Fam Physician. 2003 Aug;32(8):607-11. PMID: 12973867. Accessed at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/36d191bd-dc19-4e12-bcae-f8119a19a81e/atta
